ethical_notes: Pre-1958 European sources used the postal romanization 'Peking' (or 'Pekin'), largely derived from Nanjing-dialect renderings propagated during 19th-century Western contact. The 1958 PRC adoption of Hanyu Pinyin produced 'Beijing' as the official romanization, and UN and ISO systems followed from 1977–1986. 'Peking' persists residually in institutional names (Peking University) and Anglophone idiom but reflects an external transcription convention, not the Mandarin endonym.

sources.citation: Naquin, Susan. 'Peking: Temples and City Life, 1400–1900.' University of California Press, 2000.
Twitchett, Denis & Fairbank, John K. (eds.). 'The Cambridge History of China, Vol. 7: The Ming Dynasty, 1368–1644, Part 1.' Cambridge University Press, 1988.

name_variants.name: Khanbaliq/汗八里
name_variants.context: Mongol Yuan dynasty capital name, 'City of the Khan'; Marco Polo's 'Cambaluc'

name_variants.name: Dadu/大都
name_variants.context: Chinese name of Yuan capital, 'Great Capital'

name_variants.name: Peking
name_variants.context: Postal romanization used by European powers, derived from southern Mandarin dialect

name_variants.name: Beiping/北平
name_variants.context: ROC renaming ('Northern Peace') after capital moved to Nanjing, reversed on PRC founding

ethical_notes: Under the Tang (618–907) Chang'an was arguably the world's largest urban agglomeration, terminus of the Silk Road and home to Sogdian, Persian, Jewish, and Nestorian Christian communities. The modern name 'Xi'an' (西安, 'Western Peace') dates to the Ming (1369) renaming and erases the Tang-era prestige association; historiography continues to use 'Chang'an' specifically for the imperial city to preserve that continuity.

sources.citation: Xiong, Victor Cunrui. 'Sui-Tang Chang'an: A Study in the Urban History of Medieval China.' University of Michigan Center for Chinese Studies, 2000.
Twitchett, Denis (ed.). 'The Cambridge History of China, Vol. 3: Sui and T'ang China, 589–906, Part 1.' Cambridge University Press, 1979.

name_variants.name: Xi'an/西安
name_variants.context: Ming-era renaming, 'Western Peace'; current official name

name_variants.name: Hao/鎬
name_variants.context: Western Zhou capital name

name_variants.name: Sian
name_variants.context: Wade-Giles / postal romanization used in Western scholarship

ethical_notes: Sacked multiple times — most catastrophically by Dong Zhuo (190 CE) who forced the population to Chang'an and burned the city, and again during the Yongjia disaster (311 CE) during which Xiongnu forces massacred an estimated 30,000 imperial officials and nobles. These events represent major cultural and demographic ruptures that Han-centric historiography has frequently sanitized.

sources.citation: Twitchett, Denis & Loewe, Michael (eds.). 'The Cambridge History of China, Vol. 1: The Ch'in and Han Empires, 221 BC–AD 220.' Cambridge University Press, 1986.
Lewis, Mark Edward. 'China Between Empires: The Northern and Southern Dynasties.' Belknap Press, 2009.

name_variants.name: Luoyi/雒邑
name_variants.context: Western Zhou-era name

name_variants.name: Honan-fu
name_variants.context: Qing-era prefecture name used in Western sources

ethical_notes: Site of the Nanjing Massacre (December 1937 – January 1938), during which Imperial Japanese Army forces systematically murdered an estimated 200,000–300,000 Chinese civilians and disarmed soldiers and raped tens of thousands of women. The massacre is denied or minimized in parts of Japanese revisionist historiography and remains a primary axis of Sino-Japanese historical contention. AtlasPI represents documented Chinese, Japanese, and international (Rabe diaries, ICTFE tribunal records) sources concordantly rather than privileging any single national narrative.

sources.citation: Chang, Iris. 'The Rape of Nanking: The Forgotten Holocaust of World War II.' Basic Books, 1997.
Mote, Frederick W. 'Imperial China 900–1800.' Harvard University Press, 1999.
Fogel, Joshua A. (ed.). 'The Nanjing Massacre in History and Historiography.' University of California Press, 2000.

name_variants.name: Jiankang/建康
name_variants.context: Six Dynasties capital name

name_variants.name: Jinling/金陵
name_variants.context: Ancient and literary name, still in poetic use

name_variants.name: Nanking
name_variants.context: Postal romanization; persists in 'Rape of Nanking' historiographical shorthand

sources.citation: Gernet, Jacques. 'Daily Life in China on the Eve of the Mongol Invasion, 1250–1276.' Stanford University Press, 1962.
Twitchett, Denis & Smith, Paul Jakov (eds.). 'The Cambridge History of China, Vol. 5, Part 1: The Sung Dynasty and its Precursors, 907–1279.' Cambridge University Press, 2009.

name_variants.name: Lin'an/臨安
name_variants.context: Southern Song capital name, 'Temporary Peace' — signalling irredentist hope of recovering the north

name_variants.name: Quinsai/Kinsai
name_variants.context: Marco Polo's name for the city, from Chinese 'Xingzai' (行在, 'temporary residence')

name_variants.name: Hangchow
name_variants.context: Postal romanization

ethical_notes: Site of the Guangzhou massacre of 878–879 during Huang Chao's rebellion, in which a reported 120,000–200,000 foreign residents (Arabs, Persians, Jews, Zoroastrians, Nestorian Christians) were killed — recorded in the Arab account of Abu Zayd al-Sirafi. The European exonym 'Canton' derives from Portuguese phonetic corruption of 'Guangdong' (the province) and was the operative name of the city in Western commercial, colonial and opium-trade contexts until the pinyin transition.

sources.citation: Abu Zayd al-Sirafi (10th c.), trans. Mackintosh-Smith, Tim. 'Accounts of China and India.' NYU Press / Library of Arabic Literature, 2014.
Van Dyke, Paul A. 'The Canton Trade: Life and Enterprise on the China Coast, 1700–1845.' Hong Kong University Press, 2007.

name_variants.name: Panyu/番禺
name_variants.context: Qin-Han era name

name_variants.name: Canton
name_variants.context: Portuguese-derived European exonym used during trade and colonial periods

name_variants.name: Khanfu/خانفو
name_variants.context: Medieval Arab geographers' name

ethical_notes: Medieval Quanzhou hosted one of the largest multi-religious cosmopolitan communities in the premodern world — Arab and Persian Muslims, Tamil Hindus, Nestorian Christians, Manichaeans, and Jews left epigraphic evidence. This pluralism ended violently during the Ispah Rebellion (1357–1366), when Shi'a Persian garrisons revolted and Sunni merchant clans reprisals killed thousands; the Yuan-Ming transition consolidated the suppression, and many foreign communities were expelled or forcibly assimilated.

sources.citation: So, Billy K. L. 'Prosperity, Region and Institutions in Maritime China: The South Fukien Pattern, 946–1368.' Harvard University Asia Center, 2000.
Schottenhammer, Angela (ed.). 'The Emporium of the World: Maritime Quanzhou, 1000–1400.' Brill, 2001.

name_variants.name: Zayton/Zaitun
name_variants.context: Arab and European medieval name (Marco Polo, Ibn Battuta); possibly origin of English 'satin'

name_variants.name: Ch'üan-chou
name_variants.context: Wade-Giles romanization

ethical_notes: The Mogao 'Library Cave' manuscripts (sealed c. 1000 CE, rediscovered 1900) were largely removed between 1907–1914 by foreign expeditions — Aurel Stein (British), Paul Pelliot (French), Sergey Oldenburg (Russian), and Otani Kozui (Japanese) — often through coercive or exploitative dealings with the custodian monk Wang Yuanlu. Roughly 40,000 documents are now dispersed across London, Paris, Saint Petersburg, Beijing, Kyoto, and elsewhere; return requests from China remain unresolved. The founding year reflects the Han establishment of the commandery; the oasis was inhabited earlier by Yuezhi and Wusun peoples.

sources.citation: Hansen, Valerie. 'The Silk Road: A New History.' Oxford University Press, 2012.
Whitfield, Susan. 'Aurel Stein on the Silk Road.' British Museum Press, 2004.
Rong, Xinjiang. 'Eighteen Lectures on Dunhuang.' Brill, 2013.

name_variants.name: Shazhou/沙州
name_variants.context: Tang-Song administrative name, 'Sand Prefecture'

name_variants.name: Tun-huang
name_variants.context: Wade-Giles romanization used in Silk Road scholarship

ethical_notes: Non-Han Shu / Ba cultures occupied the Chengdu Plain long before Qin conquest (316 BCE); the Sanxingdui bronzes (c. -1200) evidence a sophisticated civilization distinct from the Yellow River core. Traditional Chinese historiography long treated the region as peripheral until archaeological work from the 1980s forced revision. Qing-era Manchu conquest (1647) and the subsequent Zhang Xianzhong massacres depopulated Sichuan so severely that the 'Huguang fills Sichuan' state-directed migration reshaped regional demography.

sources.citation: Sage, Steven F. 'Ancient Sichuan and the Unification of China.' SUNY Press, 1992.
Von Glahn, Richard. 'The Country of Streams and Grottoes: Expansion, Settlement, and the Civilizing of the Sichuan Frontier in Song Times.' Harvard University Asia Center, 1987.

name_variants.name: Yizhou/益州
name_variants.context: Han-era provincial name

name_variants.name: Chengtu
name_variants.context: Wade-Giles romanization

ethical_notes: Home to one of the oldest documented Jewish communities in China (the Kaifeng Jews, likely arrived via Silk Road routes c. 10th–12th c.). The community maintained a synagogue until the mid-19th century; after the 1642 Yellow River flood (deliberately breached by Ming forces against the Li Zicheng rebellion, killing an estimated 300,000 residents) and repeated later disasters, the community gradually assimilated. The 1642 flood is a state-inflicted mass death that Chinese historiography has sometimes framed neutrally as a 'natural' disaster.

sources.citation: Kracke, E. A., Jr. 'Sung K'ai-feng: Pragmatic Metropolis and Formalistic Capital.' In Haeger, J. W. (ed.), 'Crisis and Prosperity in Sung China', University of Arizona Press, 1975.
Xu, Xin. 'The Jews of Kaifeng, China: History, Culture, and Religion.' KTAV Publishing, 2003.

name_variants.name: Bianjing/汴京
name_variants.context: Northern Song capital name

name_variants.name: Daliang/大梁
name_variants.context: Warring States Wei-kingdom capital name

name_variants.name: Kaifung
name_variants.context: Postal romanization

ethical_notes: The Kashi Vishwanath temple was demolished under Aurangzeb in 1669 and the Gyanvapi mosque built on the site, a fact that remains a central and politically live contention in contemporary Indian historiography and legal proceedings. The temple was rebuilt adjacent to the mosque in 1780 under Ahilyabai Holkar. British administration used 'Benares', a Prakrit-derived form; the 1956 official reversion to 'Varanasi' was part of post-independence endonymic restoration. Continuous habitation since at least c. -1800 makes the city among the oldest continuously inhabited on Earth, though exact founding is uncertain.

sources.citation: Eck, Diana L. 'Banaras: City of Light.' Alfred A. Knopf, 1982.
Dalrymple, William. 'The Anarchy: The Relentless Rise of the East India Company.' Bloomsbury, 2019.
Kumar, Nita. 'The Artisans of Banaras: Popular Culture and Identity, 1880–1986.' Princeton University Press, 1988.

name_variants.name: Varanasi/वाराणसी
name_variants.context: Official post-independence name, derived from the rivers Varuna and Assi

name_variants.name: Banaras/बनारस
name_variants.context: Common vernacular name, Prakrit derivation; still in everyday use

name_variants.name: Benares
name_variants.context: British colonial anglicization; administratively replaced in 1956

ethical_notes: Megasthenes' 'Indica' (c. -300), preserved via Greek and Roman excerpts, described Pataliputra as larger than contemporary Mediterranean cities — yet this testimony filtered through Hellenistic intermediaries was long dismissed in colonial-era European scholarship. The modern name 'Patna' is a contraction that survived Mughal and British rule; 'Azimabad' (Mughal) is generally absent from Hindu-nationalist historiography that emphasizes Mauryan-Buddhist continuity over the Islamic interlude.

sources.citation: Thapar, Romila. 'Aśoka and the Decline of the Mauryas.' Oxford University Press, 3rd ed., 2012.
Allchin, F. R. 'The Archaeology of Early Historic South Asia: The Emergence of Cities and States.' Cambridge University Press, 1995.

name_variants.name: Palibothra
name_variants.context: Greek rendering used by Megasthenes and later Hellenistic geographers

name_variants.name: Azimabad/عظیم‌آباد
name_variants.context: Mughal-era name under Azim-us-Shan, grandson of Aurangzeb

name_variants.name: Patna/पटना
name_variants.context: Current name, derived from 'Pattana' (Prakrit for 'town')

ethical_notes: Takṣaśilā / Taxila hosted one of the earliest known university-like institutions (c. -500 onward), teaching Vedas, Buddhist canon, medicine, archery, and political science — predating Nalanda by a millennium. Colonial archaeology (Cunningham, Marshall) framed the Gandharan Greco-Buddhist art as evidence of 'civilizing' Hellenistic contact, marginalizing indigenous intellectual contributions. The city was destroyed by Hunnic invasions c. 460–500 CE, effectively ending its cultural continuity.

sources.citation: Marshall, John. 'Taxila: An Illustrated Account of Archaeological Excavations.' 3 vols., Cambridge University Press, 1951.
Scharfe, Hartmut. 'Education in Ancient India.' Brill, 2002.

name_variants.name: Taxila
name_variants.context: Greek rendering via Alexander's campaign historians; current English name

name_variants.name: Takkasilā/𑀢𑀔𑁆𑀔𑀲𑀺𑀮𑀸
name_variants.context: Pali form in Buddhist canonical texts

ethical_notes: Seat of the Tamil Sangam academies (tradition ascribes three Sangams over millennia; historically datable c. -300 to 300 CE), among the oldest documented literary academies in the world. The Delhi Sultanate's Malik Kafur sacked Madurai in 1311, looting the Meenakshi temple; Sangam Tamil historiography and North-Indian-centered narratives of Delhi-Sultanate conquest often do not converse. Portuguese-era English 'Madura' appears in 17th–19th-century records; current spelling follows Tamil endonym.

sources.citation: Zvelebil, Kamil V. 'Tamil Literature.' Brill, Handbuch der Orientalistik, 1975.
Stein, Burton. 'Peasant State and Society in Medieval South India.' Oxford University Press, 1980.

name_variants.name: Madura
name_variants.context: Portuguese and British colonial rendering

name_variants.name: Kūdal/கூடல்
name_variants.context: Alternate classical Tamil name, 'confluence'

ethical_notes: After the Battle of Talikota (1565), a coalition of Deccan Sultanates (Bijapur, Ahmadnagar, Golconda, Bidar) defeated Vijayanagara and subjected the city to five months of systematic destruction, massacre, and looting — one of the most thorough urban sackings in South Asian history. Colonial-era historiography (Sewell's 'A Forgotten Empire', 1900) framed Vijayanagara as a Hindu bulwark against Islamic conquest, a reading instrumentalized by contemporary Hindu nationalism; scholarly revisionism emphasizes the empire's own use of Islamic administrative and military forms and its Muslim officers. The ruins (Hampi) are a UNESCO site.

sources.citation: Sewell, Robert. 'A Forgotten Empire (Vijayanagar): A Contribution to the History of India.' Swan Sonnenschein, 1900.
Stein, Burton. 'Vijayanagara.' The New Cambridge History of India, vol. I.2. Cambridge University Press, 1989.
Wagoner, Phillip B. '"Sultan among Hindu Kings": Dress, Titles, and the Islamicization of Hindu Culture at Vijayanagara.' Journal of Asian Studies 55(4), 1996, pp. 851–880.

name_variants.name: Hampi/ಹಂಪಿ
name_variants.context: Kannada name of the ruin site and nearby village, current place-name

name_variants.name: Bijanagar
name_variants.context: Persian / Deccan Sultanate rendering

ethical_notes: Delhi has been sacked more systematically than any other major world city: Timur (1398, c. 100,000 killed), Nadir Shah (1739, 20,000–30,000 massacred in the qatl-e-aam, Peacock Throne looted), British recapture after 1857 uprising (mass executions, exile of the last Mughal emperor Bahadur Shah Zafar, razing of Mughal neighborhoods for cantonments). The 1947 Partition produced large-scale communal violence in Delhi, with Sikh/Hindu refugee influx and displacement/killing of Muslim residents — estimated 20,000–25,000 killed in the city, many in organized attacks. The 1984 anti-Sikh pogrom after Indira Gandhi's assassination killed an estimated 2,700 in Delhi, largely uncompensated legally.

sources.citation: Dalrymple, William. 'The Last Mughal: The Fall of a Dynasty, Delhi, 1857.' Bloomsbury, 2006.
Gupta, Narayani. 'Delhi Between Two Empires, 1803–1931: Society, Government, and Urban Growth.' Oxford University Press, 1981.
Kaur, Ravinder. 'Since 1947: Partition Narratives among Punjabi Migrants of Delhi.' Oxford University Press, 2007.

name_variants.name: Indraprastha/इन्द्रप्रस्थ
name_variants.context: Mahabharata-era legendary name, identified archaeologically with the Purana Qila site

name_variants.name: Shahjahanabad/شاہجہان آباد
name_variants.context: Mughal walled city of Old Delhi, built by Shah Jahan

name_variants.name: New Delhi
name_variants.context: British imperial capital inaugurated 1931, replacing Calcutta

ethical_notes: Operation Searchlight (25–26 March 1971) launched the Bangladesh genocide with a systematic massacre in Dhaka, beginning at Dhaka University, Rajarbagh Police Lines, and Hindu neighborhoods — estimates of killings over nine months of conflict range from 300,000 (Pakistani government figures) to 3,000,000 (Bangladeshi official figure), with 200,000–400,000 women raped. Pakistan has never formally acknowledged the genocide. English spelling changed from 'Dacca' to 'Dhaka' officially in 1982, a post-independence linguistic reassertion against colonial-era orthography. Mughal-era foundation as 'Jahangirnagar' 1608.

sources.citation: Bose, Sarmila. 'Dead Reckoning: Memories of the 1971 Bangladesh War.' Oxford University Press, 2011.
Jahan, Rounaq. 'Genocide in Bangladesh.' In Totten, Samuel (ed.), 'Century of Genocide'. Routledge, 3rd ed., 2009.
Ahmed, Sharif Uddin. 'Dacca: A Study in Urban History and Development.' Curzon Press, 1986.

name_variants.name: Jahangirnagar/جہانگیر نگر
name_variants.context: Mughal name after Emperor Jahangir

name_variants.name: Dacca
name_variants.context: British colonial anglicization; officially replaced 1982

ethical_notes: Calcutta was founded 1690 by Job Charnock of the English East India Company on villages (Kalikata, Sutanuti, Govindapur) acquired through leases with Mughal officials; it became the capital of British India from 1772 until 1911. The Bengal famine of 1943 killed an estimated 2.1–3 million people, with British wartime grain-requisition policies (Churchill administration) causally implicated — long omitted from Anglophone historiography before scholarship by Amartya Sen and Madhusree Mukerjee. Renaming to 'Kolkata' in 2001 was an explicit reclamation of the Bengali pronunciation against the anglicized colonial form; the city's English name remained 'Calcutta' in most international usage throughout the 20th century.

sources.citation: Chatterjee, Partha. 'The Black Hole of Empire: History of a Global Practice of Power.' Princeton University Press, 2012.
Mukerjee, Madhusree. 'Churchill's Secret War: The British Empire and the Ravaging of India During World War II.' Basic Books, 2010.
Sen, Amartya. 'Poverty and Famines: An Essay on Entitlement and Deprivation.' Oxford University Press, 1981.

name_variants.name: Calcutta
name_variants.context: British East India Company / Raj anglicization of Kalikata; official English name until the 2001 legislative rename

name_variants.name: Kalikata/কলিকাতা
name_variants.context: Original Bengali name of the pre-colonial village; etymology likely from the goddess Kali's temple at Kalighat

ethical_notes: Angkor was the largest preindustrial urban agglomeration on Earth at its peak, covering c. 1,000 km² of hydraulic-engineered landscape — a fact only established by airborne LiDAR surveys (2012, 2015) that overturned long-standing underestimates. French colonial conservation (École française d'Extrême-Orient from 1898) framed Angkor as 'lost' and 'rediscovered' by Henri Mouhot (1860), erasing the continuous local knowledge of the site maintained by Khmer Buddhist monks; some decorative elements were removed to European museums. The Khmer Rouge (1975–1979) used Angkor imagery as nationalist iconography while committing genocide against the actual Cambodian population, complicating the monument's symbolic politics.

sources.citation: Evans, Damian H., et al. 'Uncovering archaeological landscapes at Angkor using lidar.' PNAS 110(31), 2013, pp. 12595–12600.
Coedès, George. 'The Indianized States of Southeast Asia.' University of Hawai'i Press, 1968.
Higham, Charles. 'The Civilization of Angkor.' University of California Press, 2001.

name_variants.name: Angkor
name_variants.context: From Sanskrit 'nagara' (city); common current name for the archaeological complex

name_variants.name: Angkor Thom/អង្គរធំ
name_variants.context: Jayavarman VII's walled capital, 'Great City'

ethical_notes: The pre-Bamar Pyu city-states (Sri Ksetra, Halin, Beikthano) preceded Bagan on the Irrawaddy plain by centuries; Burmese nationalist historiography has at times elided this non-Bamar substratum. The 1287 Mongol invasion conventionally dated as the fall of Bagan is now considered by scholars (Aung-Thwin) to have been a more limited raid, with internal political fragmentation as the primary cause of decline. Military-junta reconstruction (1990s onward) used non-historical techniques and materials on pagodas for tourism, damaging UNESCO candidacy until 2019. Extensive damage in the 2016 earthquake; 2025 Mandalay earthquake (March 2025) caused further damage to standing structures.

sources.citation: Aung-Thwin, Michael. 'Pagan: The Origins of Modern Burma.' University of Hawai'i Press, 1985.
Stadtner, Donald M. 'Ancient Pagan: Buddhist Plain of Merit.' River Books, 2005.

name_variants.name: Pagan
name_variants.context: British colonial and early post-independence English spelling

name_variants.name: Arimaddanapura/अरिमर्दनपुर
name_variants.context: Sanskritic chronicle name, 'City of the Enemy-Crusher'

entity_name_original: อาณาจักรอยุธยา
ethical_notes: Sacked and destroyed by the Burmese Konbaung dynasty in April 1767 after a 14-month siege; population enslaved or killed, royal library and records burned — a rupture so total that Thai national memory treats it as the defining national trauma. The episode remains a live factor in Thai-Myanmar diplomatic and cultural relations. European accounts of 17th-century Ayutthaya (French, Dutch, Persian, Japanese embassies) make it one of the best-documented Southeast Asian capitals of the period; these external sources preserved details lost in the 1767 destruction.

sources.citation: Baker, Chris & Pasuk Phongpaichit. 'A History of Ayutthaya: Siam in the Early Modern World.' Cambridge University Press, 2017.
Tarling, Nicholas (ed.). 'The Cambridge History of Southeast Asia, Vol. 1: From Early Times to c. 1800.' Cambridge University Press, 1992.

name_variants.name: Ayodhya
name_variants.context: Sanskritic referent to the Ramayana city, signalling dynastic legitimacy

name_variants.name: Iudia / Odia
name_variants.context: Dutch VOC-era spelling in European correspondence

ethical_notes: Trowulan (the identified site of the Majapahit capital) is the only known pre-Islamic Javanese imperial capital visible archaeologically; Dutch colonial archaeology first mapped it systematically in the 19th century. Indonesian post-independence nationalist narrative (Sukarno onward) repositioned Majapahit as a 'proto-Indonesian' territorial empire and Gajah Mada's 'Sumpah Palapa' as a unification oath — projecting modern state boundaries anachronistically. Actual extent of Majapahit's imperial control versus tributary/commercial relationships remains academically contested; the 'Nagarakretagama' (1365) is the primary indigenous source but is a court panegyric.

sources.citation: Hall, Kenneth R. 'A History of Early Southeast Asia: Maritime Trade and Societal Development, 100–1500.' Rowman & Littlefield, 2011.
Pigeaud, Theodore G. Th. 'Java in the 14th Century: A Study in Cultural History.' 5 vols., Nijhoff, 1960–1963.

name_variants.name: Majapahit
name_variants.context: Modern Indonesian standard spelling

name_variants.name: Trowulan
name_variants.context: Current village name on the archaeological site

ethical_notes: Malacca was the first major Southeast Asian polity conquered by a European power: Portuguese forces under Afonso de Albuquerque seized it 1511 after 40 days of siege; the Portuguese conquistadors massacred much of the Muslim population and burned mosques and the royal library — a foundational act of European colonization in Asia. Subsequent Dutch (1641) and British (1824) takeovers continued the colonial sequence. The Islamic sultanate's role in spreading Islam across the archipelago is often minimized in colonial and early nationalist historiographies; the Sejarah Melayu (Malay Annals, c. 1612) remains the primary indigenous chronicle.

sources.citation: Andaya, Leonard Y. 'Leaves of the Same Tree: Trade and Ethnicity in the Straits of Melaka.' University of Hawai'i Press, 2008.
Reid, Anthony. 'Southeast Asia in the Age of Commerce, 1450–1680.' 2 vols., Yale University Press, 1988–1993.
Thomaz, Luís Filipe F. R. 'Early Portuguese Malacca.' CTMCDP / Macau, 2000.

name_variants.name: Melaka
name_variants.context: Modern Malaysian standard spelling

name_variants.name: Malacca
name_variants.context: Portuguese / English colonial-era anglicization, still common internationally

ethical_notes: Hội An was built atop the Cham port of Lâm Ấp Phố (c. 2nd c. – 15th c.), itself a major Champa trading center; the Cham civilization was gradually destroyed by Vietnamese southward expansion (Nam tiến), culminating in the 1471 Vijaya massacre (an estimated 40,000–60,000 Cham killed) under Lê Thánh Tông. Post-Cham reconstruction of Hội An by Nguyễn lords and Chinese/Japanese merchant communities almost completely erased the Cham substrate; Cham people survive as a minority in Vietnam with limited recognition. Hội An declined after 1830 when silting favored Đà Nẵng and French colonial administration developed the rival port.

sources.citation: Wheeler, Charles. 'One Region, Two Histories: Cham Precedents in the History of the Hội An Region.' In Tran, Nhung Tuyet & Reid, Anthony (eds.), 'Việt Nam: Borderless Histories', University of Wisconsin Press, 2006.
Li Tana. 'Nguyễn Cochinchina: Southern Vietnam in the Seventeenth and Eighteenth Centuries.' Cornell SEAP, 1998.

name_variants.name: Faifo / Faifoo
name_variants.context: Portuguese and Japanese trading-era name used in European sources

name_variants.name: Lâm Ấp Phố
name_variants.context: Cham-era port, Sino-Vietnamese transcription

ethical_notes: Palembang is identified as the likely core of Srivijaya (7th–13th c.), a thalassocratic Buddhist empire controlling the Malacca and Sunda straits; Chinese (Yijing) and Arabic accounts document it, but the city-scale archaeology is limited because the riverine swamp environment and later Islamization obscured continuity. The Dutch colonial sultanate takeover (1821) ended the Palembang Sultanate after the Palembang War. Srivijaya's reconstruction as proto-national heritage post-1945 sometimes anachronizes modern Indonesian boundaries onto a medieval polity whose core-periphery dynamics remain academically debated.

sources.citation: Wolters, O. W. 'Early Indonesian Commerce: A Study of the Origins of Srivijaya.' Cornell University Press, 1967.
Manguin, Pierre-Yves. 'Palembang and Sriwijaya: An Early Malay Harbour-City Rediscovered.' Journal of the Malaysian Branch of the Royal Asiatic Society 66(1), 1993.

name_variants.name: Śrīvijaya
name_variants.context: Sanskrit name of the empire-state centered here

name_variants.name: Sanfoqi/三佛齊
name_variants.context: Song-era Chinese name for the Srivijaya polity

ethical_notes: The Ōnin War (1467–1477) devastated Heian-kyō; roughly two-thirds of the city was burned, and major aristocratic and Buddhist cultural patrimony was destroyed. During WWII, Kyoto was on early atomic-bomb target shortlists; US Secretary of War Henry Stimson removed it specifically on cultural-heritage grounds, a decision that spared the city but redirected destruction to Hiroshima and Nagasaki. Name 'Kyōto' (京都, 'capital city') gradually replaced 'Heian-kyō' from c. 11th c.; the Meiji transfer of imperial residence to Edo/Tokyo in 1869 effectively demoted Kyoto despite its retaining the formal title 'saikyō' (西京) briefly.

sources.citation: Hall, John Whitney (ed.). 'The Cambridge History of Japan, Vol. 3: Medieval Japan.' Cambridge University Press, 1990.
Stavros, Matthew. 'Kyoto: An Urban History of Japan's Premodern Capital.' University of Hawai'i Press, 2014.
Kelly, Jason M. 'Why Did Henry Stimson Spare Kyoto from the Bomb?: Confusion in Postwar Historiography.' Journal of American-East Asian Relations 19(2), 2012.

name_variants.name: Kyōto/京都
name_variants.context: Current name, 'capital city'; in common use from late Heian

name_variants.name: Miyako/都
name_variants.context: Classical term meaning simply 'the capital'

name_variants.name: Meaco
name_variants.context: 16th–17th c. Portuguese Jesuit rendering

ethical_notes: Renamed Tōkyō (東京, 'Eastern Capital') by imperial edict 17 July 1868 (Meiji 1/7/17), following the Meiji Restoration's abolition of the Tokugawa shogunate; the emperor moved from Kyoto to occupy Edo Castle. The rename was an explicit act of political rebranding to consolidate centralized imperial rule and break with shogunal legacy. The Great Kantō earthquake (1 September 1923) killed an estimated 105,000–140,000; in the ensuing chaos, organized mobs and some police and military units massacred an estimated 6,000 ethnic Koreans and several hundred Chinese residents on false rumors — a state-implicated atrocity formally acknowledged by the Japanese government only partially and episodically. US firebombing 9–10 March 1945 killed an estimated 100,000 civilians in a single night, the most destructive conventional air raid in history.

sources.citation: Seidensticker, Edward. 'Low City, High City: Tokyo from Edo to the Earthquake.' Alfred A. Knopf, 1983.
Ryan, Lyndall. 'The 1923 Kanto Earthquake and the Korean Massacres.' In Totten, Samuel (ed.), 'Dirty Hands and Vicious Deeds', University of Toronto Press, 2018.
Jansen, Marius B. 'The Making of Modern Japan.' Belknap Press, 2000.

name_variants.name: Tōkyō/東京
name_variants.context: Imperial rebranding 17 July 1868 (Meiji era); 'Eastern Capital'

name_variants.name: Yedo / Jeddo
name_variants.context: Early-modern European (especially Dutch-derived) rendering used in pre-Meiji sources

sources.citation: Brown, Delmer M. (ed.). 'The Cambridge History of Japan, Vol. 1: Ancient Japan.' Cambridge University Press, 1993.
Piggott, Joan R. 'The Emergence of Japanese Kingship.' Stanford University Press, 1997.

name_variants.name: Heijō-kyō/平城京
name_variants.context: Formal capital name during the Nara period

ethical_notes: Capital of Koryŏ (918–1392), the dynasty from which the Western exonym 'Korea' derives. The Mongol invasions (1231–1259) devastated the city; later, during the Imjin War (1592–1598) Japanese forces occupied and damaged it. Most critically, Kaesŏng — unlike most of North Korea — was south of the 38th parallel at the 1945 partition but fell under DPRK control after the Korean War Armistice (1953) adjusted the demarcation line; it remains the only major city to have changed Korean-state affiliation. As DPRK territory, Kaesŏng has been largely closed to independent historical scholarship; UNESCO recognition of its monuments (2013) required unusual diplomatic negotiation.

sources.citation: Lee, Ki-baik. 'A New History of Korea.' Harvard University Press, 1984.
Seth, Michael J. 'A History of Korea: From Antiquity to the Present.' Rowman & Littlefield, 2010.

name_variants.name: Songdo/松都
name_variants.context: Koryŏ-era literary name, 'Pine Capital'

name_variants.name: Kaesong
name_variants.context: McCune-Reischauer romanization commonly used in English

ethical_notes: Under Japanese colonial rule (1910–1945) the city was renamed 'Keijō' (경성, 京城) by the colonial administration; systematic destruction of Korean royal palaces (including the razing of parts of Gyeongbokgung to build the Japanese Governor-General's Building) represented deliberate symbolic erasure of Chosŏn sovereignty. The Governor-General's Building itself was only demolished in 1995 as a decolonization gesture. Post-liberation 1946 rename to 'Seoul' (서울, pure-Korean word meaning 'capital', without hanja characters) was a conscious linguistic assertion of Korean identity against both Japanese and Sinitic nomenclature — 'Seoul' has no standard Chinese-character equivalent and the PRC/ROC only adopted '首尔' (Shǒu'ěr) in 2005 at Seoul's official request.

sources.citation: Cumings, Bruce. 'Korea's Place in the Sun: A Modern History.' W. W. Norton, 1997.
Henry, Todd A. 'Assimilating Seoul: Japanese Rule and the Politics of Public Space in Colonial Korea, 1910–1945.' University of California Press, 2014.

name_variants.name: Hanseong/한성
name_variants.context: Chosŏn-dynasty formal capital name, 'Han-river fortress'

name_variants.name: Keijō/京城
name_variants.context: Japanese colonial-era name imposed during annexation

name_variants.name: Seoul/서울
name_variants.context: Post-liberation official name; pure-Korean word for 'capital', no hanja equivalent

ethical_notes: Burned by Alexander of Macedon in 330 BCE — whether as deliberate retaliation for Xerxes' burning of the Athenian Acropolis or as a drunken act (as Plutarch and Diodorus variously report) is debated, but the destruction was total and ended Achaemenid Pārsa as a functioning city. Greek historiography (our primary source) systematically framed Achaemenid Persia as 'despotic' and 'Oriental' to contrast with Hellenic 'freedom' — a rhetorical frame extended into 19th–20th-century Orientalism and that shaped the Western reception of the site. The Persian name 'Pārsa' is attested in inscriptions; 'Persepolis' is Greek. Modern Iranian nationalist narratives (Pahlavi and post-revolutionary) instrumentalize the site distinctly.

sources.citation: Briant, Pierre. 'From Cyrus to Alexander: A History of the Persian Empire.' Eisenbrauns, 2002 (French orig. 1996).
Curtis, John & Tallis, Nigel (eds.). 'Forgotten Empire: The World of Ancient Persia.' British Museum Press, 2005.

name_variants.name: Persepolis/Περσέπολις
name_variants.context: Greek historiographic name, 'City of the Persians'; origin of all Western names

name_variants.name: Takht-e Jamshid/تخت جمشید
name_variants.context: Medieval-to-modern Persian name, 'Throne of Jamshid' (legendary Iranian king)

ethical_notes: Chinggis Khan's army sacked Samarqand in March 1220; medieval Persian chroniclers (Juvaini, Rashid al-Din) describe the population systematically massacred or enslaved, with artisans spared for deportation to Mongolia — casualty estimates range from tens of thousands to over a hundred thousand. The pre-Mongol Sogdian civilization that made Samarqand a Silk Road heart was substantially erased. Timur rebuilt the city as his imperial capital (1370–1405), forcibly transplanting craftsmen from conquered territories (Damascus, Delhi, Tabriz) — itself a population-engineering project. Soviet-era archaeological work (Masson, Belenitskii) recovered much Sogdian heritage but also operated within a Russocentric framework.

sources.citation: Frye, Richard N. 'The Heritage of Central Asia: From Antiquity to the Turkish Expansion.' Markus Wiener, 1996.
Manz, Beatrice Forbes. 'The Rise and Rule of Tamerlane.' Cambridge University Press, 1989.
Juvaini, Ata-Malik (13th c.), trans. Boyle, J. A. 'The History of the World-Conqueror.' Manchester University Press, 1958.

name_variants.name: Maracanda/Μαράκανδα
name_variants.context: Hellenistic name from Alexander's campaign records

name_variants.name: Afrāsiyāb/افراسیاب
name_variants.context: Pre-Mongol Sogdian settlement site name, now the archaeological mound

ethical_notes: Samanid-era Bukhara (9th–10th c.) was the intellectual capital of the Persianate Islamic world — Ibn Sina (Avicenna), al-Bukhari (compiler of Sahih al-Bukhari hadith collection), al-Biruni studied or worked here. Chinggis Khan's 1220 sack included the destruction of the Kalon Mosque and the reported massacre of residents sheltering in the citadel. Soviet incorporation (1920, conquest of the Bukhara Emirate by the Red Army under Frunze) ended the last Uzbek-Persian emirate; Soviet historiography reframed this as 'liberation from feudal oppression' while repressing Islamic scholarship and Persian-language education. The 1930s Soviet purges eliminated much of the traditional clerical and scholarly class.

sources.citation: Frye, Richard N. 'Bukhara: The Medieval Achievement.' University of Oklahoma Press, 1965.
Narshakhi (10th c.), trans. Frye, R. N. 'The History of Bukhara.' Markus Wiener, 2007.
Khalid, Adeeb. 'Islam after Communism: Religion and Politics in Central Asia.' University of California Press, 2007.

name_variants.name: Buxoro
name_variants.context: Uzbek post-Soviet Latin-script spelling

name_variants.name: Bokhara
name_variants.context: Common 19th–early-20th-c. English rendering (Great Game era)

ethical_notes: Safavid capital under Shah Abbas I from 1598 ('Isfahan nesf-e jahan', 'Isfahan is half the world'); the urban design (Naqsh-e Jahan square) is a landmark of Islamic city-planning. Shah Abbas's 1604 deportation of tens of thousands of Armenians from Julfa (on the Aras) to New Julfa (south of Isfahan) was a coercive population transfer that integrated Armenian commercial networks into Safavid silk trade — beneficent in some historiography, forced displacement in Armenian memory. The 1722 Afghan Ghilji siege and sack of Isfahan ended the Safavid dynasty; much of the city's population starved during the six-month siege, and the subsequent destruction marked a demographic and cultural rupture.

sources.citation: Blake, Stephen P. 'Half the World: The Social Architecture of Safavid Isfahan, 1590–1722.' Mazda Publishers, 1999.
Matthee, Rudi. 'Persia in Crisis: Safavid Decline and the Fall of Isfahan.' I. B. Tauris, 2012.
Aslanian, Sebouh David. 'From the Indian Ocean to the Mediterranean: The Global Trade Networks of Armenian Merchants from New Julfa.' University of California Press, 2011.

name_variants.name: Sepahan/Spahan
name_variants.context: Pre-Islamic Middle Persian name, 'place of armies'

name_variants.name: Ispahan
name_variants.context: Early-modern European rendering via French

ethical_notes: Arsacid and Sassanid imperial capital on the Tigris; sacked repeatedly by Roman armies (Trajan 116, Septimius Severus 197, Carus 283, Julian's failed campaign 363) and finally taken by the Rashidun Arab conquest in 637 (Battle of al-Qādisiyya, subsequent occupation). The Arab sack ended Sassanid Iran and triggered mass conversion to Islam over the following centuries; the Sassanid Avesta-Zoroastrian scholarly apparatus was substantially destroyed or dispersed. The city was progressively abandoned as the Abbasid foundation of Baghdad (762) drew population north; the Taq Kasra arch remains as one of the largest surviving brick vaults of antiquity. Iraq's recent conflicts (2003– ) have threatened what remains of the site.

sources.citation: Daryaee, Touraj. 'Sasanian Persia: The Rise and Fall of an Empire.' I. B. Tauris, 2009.
Kröger, Jens. 'Sasanian Remains from Ctesiphon.' In Encyclopaedia Iranica, vol. VI, 1993.
Morony, Michael G. 'Iraq after the Muslim Conquest.' Princeton University Press, 1984.

name_variants.name: Ctesiphon/Κτησιφῶν
name_variants.context: Greek / Latin historiographic name, canonical in Western sources

name_variants.name: al-Madā'in/المدائن
name_variants.context: Arabic name, 'The Cities' (referring to the Ctesiphon metropolitan complex of multiple adjacent urban centers)
